title: Fewer Fridays
passage:
When the software firm Aldermere told its staff in the spring of 2021 that they would keep their full salaries but surrender one working day a week, the response from the rest of the industry was one of near-universal ___(1)___. Competitors assumed the scheme was a recruitment stunt, the sort of gesture a company quietly drops the moment its order book fills up again. A few were blunter still. They predicted that output would ___(2)___ within a single quarter, once the charm of three-day weekends had faded and the same volume of work had to be forced into fewer hours.

Two years on, that forecast looks hasty. Measured against the same months of the previous year, output per employee did not fall at all; it edged upward by a few percentage points, and the days lost to sickness roughly halved. The independent consultancy that audited the figures, Harlen & Voss, put the net change in productivity at a little over three per cent. The result was not, for all that, an unqualified success. ___(3)___, the gains were concentrated in two of the firm's seven departments, and Aldermere's founder, Mira Aldous, is the first to say so. She remains notably ___(4)___ about how far the numbers can be stretched. "Take away our unusually settled list of clients," she cautions, "and the same year might have read very differently."

That restraint is worth heeding, because the four-day week is already being marketed to other employers as though the verdict were in. It is not. The conditions that made Aldermere's experiment work — deep reserves, patient clients, a young and portable workforce — are precisely the ones that most firms lack. Whether an arrangement that flatters a small, loyal, comfortably financed software house is genuinely ___(5)___ to a factory floor or a hospital ward is a question that no single cheering trial, Aldermere's included, can settle on its own.

questions.rationale: Suffix-rhymed -ision set. The frame 'the response ... was one of near-universal ___', glossed by the next sentences (rivals dismiss the scheme as a stunt), needs a noun naming widespread ridicule/scorn: 'near-universal derision' is the locking collocation. revision = collocation_misfit (no idiomatic 'universal revision' reaction reading). provision = sense misfit (a supply/clause, not a reaction). precision = sense misfit (accuracy, not a response). Only 'derision' is idiomatic in context; the other three are real -ision nouns that fail on collocation/sense.

Polarity gap. The critics forecast a fall once 'the same volume of work had to be forced into fewer hours', and paragraph 2 reverses it ('output ... did not fall at all'); so the predicted verb must be negative-pole: output would slump. surge = polarity_mirror (the positive pole a skimmer grabs, but critics would not predict a rise as a criticism). stabilise = sense/polarity error (that is roughly what actually happened, not the feared collapse, and no critic 'predicts' stabilise as a warning). wither = collocation_misfit ('output withers' is not idiomatic; wither takes plants, limbs, enthusiasm). Only 'slump' fits the negative forecast.

Connective-logic gap; all four are sentence adverbs (connective class matched). The prior sentence concedes the trial 'was not ... an unqualified success', and the gapped sentence supplies confirming evidence (gains confined to two of seven departments). That relationship is reinforcement, so 'Indeed' is the only fit. However = wrong logic (signals contrast, but the clause supports rather than opposes the concession). Conversely = wrong logic (demands an opposite, which is not offered). Otherwise = wrong logic (conditional 'if not', absent here). Only 'Indeed' preserves the reinforcing relation.

Suffix-rhymed -ent set. Aldous is 'the first to say so' and immediately hedges her own result, so she 'remains notably ___ about how far the numbers can be stretched' = guarded/unwilling to overclaim: reticent ('reticent about X' is the idiom). insistent = polarity_mirror (the opposite pole; a skimmer expecting a confident founder grabs it, but the sentence shows caution, not assertion). consistent = collocation_misfit ('consistent about how far' has no idiomatic reading). prevalent = sense misfit (describes how widespread a thing is, cannot describe a person's stance). Only 'reticent' fits.

Suffix-rhymed -able set; all four take 'to X' grammatically, so the choice turns on collocation, not form. The clause asks whether a model that suits one firm is genuinely ___ 'to a factory floor or a hospital ward' = can be carried across to those settings: transferable ('transferable to' is the idiom for generalising a result). answerable = sense misfit ('answerable to' means accountable to a person/body, not applicable to a setting). agreeable = sense misfit ('agreeable to' means acceptable/pleasing to someone, not generalisable). amenable = sense misfit ('amenable to' means open/tractable, wrong of a result offered to a workplace). Only 'transferable' is idiomatic here.

generator_meta.gap_marker_convention: ___(n)___ inline in passage; questions[] carries gap n as q_index n with prompt 'Gap (n)'
